food_name: Banh Xeo (Vietnamese Sizzling Rice Flour Crepe)
food_subgroup: Shallow-fried rice flour crepe with pork belly, shrimp, and bean sprouts; served with lettuce wraps and nuoc cham dipping sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Banh Xeo specifically. Closest parent would be a rice flour pancake or savoury crepe category. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion for Southeast Asian dishes.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice flour crepe with pork and shrimp — closest parent match in FoodEx2; no exact code for Banh Xeo exists
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GMF used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a rice-flour-based savoury crepe with pork and shrimp filling. Exact Banh Xeo code does not exist in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Banh Xeo or Vietnamese sizzling crepe as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 full crepe (~300g, includes filling, excludes lettuce and herbs for wrapping)
serving.natural_language_portion: One full Banh Xeo crepe weighs approximately 300g including the rice flour batter, pork belly, shrimp, and bean sprout filling; lettuce leaves and fresh herbs used for wrapping are not included in this weight.

nutrient_source.title: Banh Xeo — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table 2020 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 for the rice flour crepe base and coconut milk component, combined with USDA SR Legacy values for pork belly, shrimp, and bean sprouts. Component-level calculation performed to arrive at composite per-100g values for the assembled dish. This is an adapted/estimated match as no single approved-source entry for the complete Banh Xeo dish exists.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice flour-based foods — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice flour crepe entries; moderated by coconut milk fat content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Banh Xeo exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 estimated from rice flour crepe analogues (GI range 60–72 for rice flour-based pancakes/crepes). Coconut milk fat in the batter provides genuine GI moderation relative to plain rice flour products. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 40g) / 100 = 26.0. Confidence: 0.9 (analogous estimate from approved GI source).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 283mg per 100g; ~850mg per full crepe with standard nuoc cham (50ml); ~350mg per crepe without nuoc cham
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid nuoc cham dipping sauce, as the sauce contributes approximately 500mg of the 850mg total sodium per serving; consuming the crepe without nuoc cham reduces sodium to a moderate ~350mg per serving, making it one of the lower-sodium Vietnamese restaurant options.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~65 (medium), GL ~26.0 per full crepe — moderate-high glycaemic load; coconut milk fat and bean sprout fibre provide partial GI moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that one full Banh Xeo crepe delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 26, which is moderate-high; the traditional lettuce-and-herb wrapping method slows eating pace and adds fibre, and limiting nuoc cham reduces sodium, making this a relatively favourable Vietnamese option when portion size is controlled to half a crepe.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and coconut milk (~2.5g per 100g; ~7.5g per full crepe); cholesterol ~50mg per 100g; sodium ~850mg per serving with nuoc cham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that one full Banh Xeo crepe provides approximately 7.5g saturated fat and 150mg cholesterol from pork belly and coconut milk, alongside high sodium when nuoc cham is included; choosing a smaller portion, requesting less pork belly, and omitting nuoc cham will meaningfully reduce saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trace curcumin from turmeric in batter; bean sprouts provide Vitamin C; shrimp provides zinc and selenium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The quantity of turmeric in Banh Xeo batter (~0.5–1g per crepe) provides only ~10–20mg curcumin, which is well below the ~500mg/day clinical threshold for anti-inflammatory benefit; however, the dish contributes zinc (2.1mg per serving) and selenium (30mcg per serving) from shrimp, which support immune function relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Xèo (Banh Xeo) is a Vietnamese sizzling crepe — one of the most iconic street foods and restaurant dishes of Central and Southern Vietnam. The name 'xèo' literally means 'sizzle', referring to the sound the rice flour batter makes when it hits the hot oiled pan. The crepe is made from a batter of rice flour, coconut milk, turmeric (which gives the crepe its characteristic bright yellow colour), and water, poured into a very hot pan and cooked until the edges are crispy and lacy. The filling — pork belly slices, whole shrimp, and bean sprouts — is placed inside and the crepe is folded in half. Banh Xeo is eaten by tearing pieces of the crepe, wrapping them in fresh lettuce leaves or mustard greens with fresh herbs (mint, perilla, Vietnamese coriander), and dipping in nuoc cham (a sweet-sour-salty fish sauce dipping sauce). Per full crepe (~300g, excluding lettuce and herbs): approximately 430 kcal, 18g protein, 18g fat, 40g carbohydrate, GI ~65 (medium), GL ~26.0, ~850mg sodium with nuoc cham (~350mg without). The coconut milk fat in the batter moderates the glycaemic index relative to plain rice flour products. Nutritionally, Banh Xeo provides moderate protein from pork belly and shrimp, moderate saturated fat, and meaningful micronutrients including zinc (2.1mg/serving), selenium (30mcg/serving), phosphorus, and B vitamins. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. The primary clinical concern is sodium when nuoc cham is used liberally, and moderate glycaemic load for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation. Confidence: medium.
meal_context: Vietnamese shared appetiser or main course

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a component-level calculation using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 and USDA SR Legacy — no single approved-source entry for the complete assembled Banh Xeo dish exists. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice flour crepe entries. The input record confidence was 0.7 (moderate), consistent with a medium confidence rating under Project Veritas v2 policy.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Banh Xeo dish (rice flour crepe + pork belly + shrimp + bean sprouts) is not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA SR Legacy; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require individual ingredient disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the assembled dish is not reported in the source tables consulted. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for this preparation method (shallow-frying in vegetable oil, no hydrogenated fats used). 3. Sodium value of 283mg per 100g reflects the assembled crepe with filling but WITHOUT nuoc cham. Per full crepe (~300g) with 50ml nuoc cham, total sodium is approximately 850mg. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice flour crepe entries; no direct Banh Xeo GI measurement exists in the approved GI database. 5. FoodEx2 code A0GMF used as closest parent — no exact FoodEx2 code for Banh Xeo exists. FoodOn URI is null — no FoodOn entry for Banh Xeo exists as of 2024. 6. Halal status is marked false as pork belly is a primary ingredient. 7. Vitamin A reported as 8.0 mcg RAE per 100g (from turmeric and bean sprouts); this is a low value consistent with the ingredient profile.
